summary: Kno2 supports reducing unnecessary regulatory burdens to enable modern interoperability and AI-enabled tools but urges caution to avoid interoperability regressions, especially for under-resourced providers relying on legacy methods like Direct. They recommend phasing out legacy criteria only after practical replacements are widely adopted and maintaining transparency into real-world interoperability performance.
key_arguments: Removing Direct-related certification criteria prematurely risks disrupting practical interoperability for providers lacking resources to adopt FHIR quickly.
Deregulatory actions should avoid interoperability regression, especially for low-margin, under-resourced providers in LTPAC and similar settings.
Reducing certification and reporting requirements may reduce visibility into real-world interoperability performance.
A FHIR-forward policy should not become FHIR-exclusive in practice during the transition period.
recommendations: Adopt a 'no interoperability regression' principle with phased or deferred removal of legacy criteria based on market readiness.
Do not finalize immediate removal of Direct-related certification criteria; if removed, require continued support for existing document exchange pathways.
Evaluate transitions-of-care revisions with outcome-based criteria and provide guidance for under-resourced settings.
Include post-finalization market monitoring to assess if deregulation shifts costs to providers.
Retain minimal but meaningful real-world interoperability measures beyond FHIR app usage.
Explicitly acknowledge need for multiple interoperable transport methods during FHIR transition.
Prioritize implementation resources and technical assistance for smaller, under-resourced providers.
notable_quote: HTI-5 should not remove practical interoperability pathways faster than the market has fully replaced them at scale.
